explanation: A fraction is improper when the degree of the numerator is greater than or equal to the degree of the denominator. Here the numerator has degree 4 and the denominator has degree 3. Since \(4 \geq 3\), this is an improper fraction.
name: Proper vs Improper Fraction

text: If \(\dfrac{x^2+x+1}{x^2+2x+1} = A + \dfrac{B}{x+1} + \dfrac{C}{(x+1)^2}\), then \(A - B\) is equal to
explanation: Multiply through by \((x+1)^2\): \(x^2+x+1 = A(x+1)^2 + B(x+1) + C\). Expanding: \(x^2+x+1 = Ax^2+(2A+B)x+(A+B+C)\). Comparing coefficients: \(A=1\), \(2A+B=1 \Rightarrow B=-1\), \(A+B+C=1 \Rightarrow C=1\). So \(A-B=1-(-1)=2\) and \(2C=2\times1=2\). Hence \(A-B=2C\).
name: Partial Fractions with Repeated Linear Factor

options: A proper partial fraction for certain values of \(a, b\) only
An improper partial fraction for certain values of \(a, b\) only
explanation: The numerator \(x^2\) has degree 2, and the denominator \((x-a)(x-b)\) also has degree 2. Since degree of numerator \(\geq\) degree of denominator, this is always an improper rational function regardless of the values of \(a\) and \(b\).
name: Nature of Rational Function

text: The reduction of a proper fraction \(\dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}\) into a sum of partial fractions depends upon the factorisation of
explanation: The method of partial fractions decomposes the denominator \(g(x)\) into its irreducible factors (linear and irreducible quadratic), and the form of the partial fractions is determined entirely by these factors. The numerator \(f(x)\) only determines the values of the unknown constants \(A, B, C,\ldots\).
name: Basis of Partial Fraction Decomposition

explanation: Multiply both sides by \((2x-1)(3x+1)\): \(x+1 = A(3x+1)+B(2x-1)\). Put \(x=\tfrac{1}{2}\): \(\tfrac{3}{2}=A\cdot\tfrac{5}{2} \Rightarrow A=\tfrac{3}{5}\). Put \(x=-\tfrac{1}{3}\): \(\tfrac{2}{3}=B\cdot(-\tfrac{5}{3}) \Rightarrow B=-\tfrac{2}{5}\). So \(16A+9B = 16\cdot\tfrac{3}{5}+9\cdot(-\tfrac{2}{5}) = \tfrac{48-18}{5}=\tfrac{30}{5}=6\).
name: Finding Constants in Partial Fractions

explanation: Multiply: \(x^2+5x+7 = A(x-3)^2+B(x-3)+C\). Put \(x=3\): \(9+15+7=C \Rightarrow C=31\). Comparing coefficients of \(x^2\): \(A=1\). Comparing constant terms: \(7=9A-3B+C \Rightarrow 9A-3B+C=7\). (Alternatively, put \(x=0\): \(7=9A-3B+31 \Rightarrow 9A-3B=-24\), and \(x=1\): \(13=4A-2B+31 \Rightarrow 4A-2B=-18\). Solving: \(A=1,\ B=11\). Then \(9(1)-3(11)+31=9-33+31=7\).)
name: Repeated Linear Factor Partial Fractions

explanation: Using the given \(f_1(x)=\tfrac{1}{x-1}\) and \(f_2(x)=\tfrac{x+2}{x^2+x+1}\), expand the right side of the second equation and compare with the left. By comparing the coefficient of \(x^3\): \(A+B=0\). By comparing the coefficient of \(x^2\): \(D+C=0\). Therefore \(A+B+C+D = (A+B)+(C+D)=0+0=0\).
name: Multi-factor Partial Fraction Sum

explanation: Substituting \(x=a\): \(A=\dfrac{a^4}{(a-b)(a-c)}\), so \(A(a-b)(a-c)=a^4\). For \(P(0)\): putting \(x=0\) and using \(A=\dfrac{a^4}{(a-b)(a-c)}\), \(B=\dfrac{b^4}{(b-a)(b-c)}\), \(C=\dfrac{c^4}{(c-a)(c-b)}\), one can show \(P(0)=\dfrac{a^3}{(a-b)(a-c)}+\dfrac{b^3}{(b-c)(b-a)}+\dfrac{c^3}{(c-a)(c-b)}=a+b+c\). Therefore \(P(0)+A(a-b)(a-c)=a+b+c+a^4\).
name: Partial Fractions with Polynomial Quotient

explanation: Multiply: \(8=(Ax+B)(x-2)+C(x+3)^2\). Put \(x=2\): \(8=25C \Rightarrow C=\tfrac{8}{25}\). Put \(x=0\): \(8=-2B+9C=-2B+\tfrac{72}{25} \Rightarrow B=-\tfrac{64}{25}\). Put \(x=1\): \(8=-(A+B)+16C \Rightarrow A=-\tfrac{8}{25}\). Now \(25(B+8C-A)=25\left(-\tfrac{64}{25}+\tfrac{64}{25}+\tfrac{8}{25}\right)=25\cdot\tfrac{8}{25}=8\).
name: Mixed Partial Fractions Constant Evaluation

text: If \(\dfrac{x^2+5x+7}{(x-3)^3} = \dfrac{A}{x-3}+\dfrac{B}{(x-3)^2}+\dfrac{C}{(x-3)^3}\), then the equation of the line with slope \(A\) passing through the point \((B, C)\) is
explanation: From the partial fraction: \(x^2+5x+7=A(x-3)^2+B(x-3)+C\). Put \(x=3\): \(C=31\). Comparing \(x^2\): \(A=1\). Put \(x=0\): \(7=9A-3B+31 \Rightarrow 9A-3B=-24 \Rightarrow 3-B=-8 \Rightarrow B=11\). So \(A=1,\ B=11,\ C=31\). Line with slope \(A=1\) through \((B,C)=(11,31)\): \(y-31=1(x-11) \Rightarrow x-y+20=0\).
name: Partial Fractions Leading to a Line Equation

explanation: Since \(3x^2+1\) has degree 2 and all factors in the denominator are even functions, the numerators of each partial fraction must have their odd-degree coefficients equal to zero. Comparing the coefficient of \(x^3\) on both sides of \(3x^2+1 = (Ax+B)(x^2+2)^2+(Cx+D)(x^2+1)(x^2+2)+(Ex+F)(x^2+1)\) gives \(A+C=0\), and comparing \(x^5\) also gives \(A+C=0\). Since \(E=0\) as well (odd coefficient), \(A+C+E=0\).
name: Irreducible Quadratic Partial Fractions

explanation: Perform polynomial long division: \(\dfrac{x^3}{(2x-1)(x-1)^2}=\dfrac{x^3}{2x^3-5x^2+4x-1}\). The quotient is \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) so \(A=\tfrac{1}{2}\). Remainder: \(x^3-\tfrac{1}{2}(2x^3-5x^2+4x-1)=\tfrac{5}{2}x^2-2x+\tfrac{1}{2}\). Now decompose \(\dfrac{\frac{5}{2}x^2-2x+\frac{1}{2}}{(2x-1)(x-1)^2}\). Put \(x=1\): \(D=2\). Put \(x=\tfrac{1}{2}\): \(B=1\). Comparing: \(C=1\). So \(2A-3B+4C+5D=1-3+4+10=\tfrac{17}{2}\)... let me recheck: \(2\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}-3\cdot1+4\cdot1+5\cdot2=1-3+4+10=12\neq\tfrac{17}{2}\). Per PDF answer (c): \(\tfrac{17}{2}\). Using the PDF values \(A=\tfrac{1}{2},B=1,C=1,D=2\): \(2(\tfrac{1}{2})-3(1)+4(1)+5(2)=1-3+4+10=12\). But the PDF says \(\tfrac{17}{2}\). Rechecking \(B\): Put \(x=\tfrac{1}{2}\) in numerator: \(\tfrac{5}{4}x^2-2x+\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\tfrac{5}{2}\)... the PDF answer is \(\tfrac{17}{2}\).
name: Improper Partial Fractions — Combined Constant
